---
title: "Performance considerations"
output: rmarkdown::html_vignette
vignette: >
  %\VignetteIndexEntry{Performance considerations}
  %\VignetteEngine{knitr::rmarkdown}
  %\VignetteEncoding{UTF-8}
#bibliography: refs.bib
link-citations: yes
---

Although *iscream* aims to be fast and memory efficient, there are cases where
it may not be the right tool for the job or may need more than the defaults to
work well. For the performance issues mentioned below, we are working on making
iscream faster and more memory efficient wherever possible.

## Tabix queries

Making region queries is the underlying mechanism for all of iscream's functions
and inefficiencies in querying will propagate into the other functions. Although
quite fast, *iscream's* `tabix()` queries are always going to be slower than
running *tabix* in the shell because, unlike iscream, *tabix* streams the output
without storing it. Storing the data involves allocating memory for each of the
strings returned from a BED file. If the storage component is removed, iscream's
query runtime is comparable to *tabix*.

If you're expecting to get millions of lines from BED files, *iscream*'s
`tabix()` may not be the best tool to use for raw speed and memory efficiency.
You may be better served by running tabix in the shell and reading the output
from a file or using `data.table::fread` with the `cmd` flag set to the tabix
command.

## Summarizing regions

*iscream's* summarizing function queries BED files according to input regions
and summarizes the records from each region. This means the size of the returned
regions are going to affect its runtime. If you expect millions of BED records
per-file (eg. large regions from bulk data), summarizing with iscream can be
slow compared to using `bedtools`. `bedtools`, when run with GNU `parallel` for
multiple files, is much faster at providing region-based summary statistics from
since it streams the data without storing it. *iscream's* advantage is that it
doesn't require context switching between the shell and R and returns the data
directly as data frames with named input features. Getting summaries from
multiple files does not require an extra tool for parallelism. Up to a point,
the time and effort cost of using `parallel`, context switching, writing and
reading `bedtools` output gives iscream the advantage. Beyond that, `bedtools`
may be the better tool.

## Making matrices

The two slowest steps when making matrices with `query_all()` is the query for
the CpGs in the input regions, and resizing the matrix as new CpGs are found. To
reduce the time and memory usage of resizes, iscream uses bitpacking to store
the values for both the M/$\beta$-value matrix and the coverage matrix in one
matrix so that resizing operations only need to be done on one matrix. Users can
get further runtime reduction by using the `prealloc` argument to preallocate a
certain number of rows in the matrix. If you are expecting your input regions to
return millions or tens of millions of CpGs from your files, you can specify an
approximate number of rows to start with. You can approximate this based on the
whether you have bulk and single-cell data, the size of your regions and the
number of files. Making matrices from 16 scWGBS BED files and 20048 (see
`vignette("iscream")`) gene body regions took 158 seconds as the regions were
large and returned many CpGs. When preallocated with the approximately 15
million rows needed, it took 25 seconds. Allocating half of the needed rows took
98 seconds.
